title: Influence of anode material composition on the stability of electrochemically-prepared ferrate(VI) solutions
abstract:
Inﬂuence of anode material composition on the
stability of electrochemically-prepared
ferrate(VI) solutions
Karel Bouzek,1 Martin J Schmidt2 and Anthony A Wragg2*
1Department of Inorganic Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology, Technicka´ 5, 16628 Prague 6, Czech Republic
2School of Engineering, University of Exeter, North Park Road, Exeter, EX4 4QF, UK
Abstract: The stability of electrochemically-prepared ferrate(VI) solution in 14M NaOH solution was
studied. White cast iron and pure iron were used as anode materials and the anodic current density
during the ferrate(VI) preparation was varied in the range 4.4 to 42.8mAcmÿ2. The solution tem-
perature ranged from 20 to 50°C. The ferrate(VI) decomposition rate was found to depend strongly on
solution temperature, anodic current density and also on the anode material composition. The de-
composition rate was higher for white cast iron ie the anode material with greater iron carbide content.
# 1999 Society of Chemical Industry
Keywords: ferrate(VI) stability; decomposition rate; temperature; anode composition; initial concentration

INTRODUCTION
As discussed in a previous paper,1 ferrate (ferrate
means ferrate(VI) throughout this text) has a wide
range of potential applications due to its strong
oxidising properties. Since ferrate is synthesised in
aqueous media, knowledge of solution stability con-
sequent to its preparation is crucial to its use in
practical situations.
Solution stability was studied intensively in the
1950s, one of the signi®cant early papers being that of
Schreyer and Ockerman.2 These authors studied the
in¯uence of the initial ferrate concentration. They
found that the ferrate decomposition rate increased
with increasing ferrate concentration and noted that a
concentration of 0.03M was critical. If the starting
concentration exceeded this value, ferrate decomposi-
tion proceeded rapidly to complete decomposition.
Otherwise, a few minutes after the commencement of
decomposition, the kinetics slowed down and re-
mained constant for several hours. The decomposition
rate
then
increased
to
complete
decomposition.
Schreyer and Ockerman also studied the in¯uence of
the addition of KCl, KNO3, NaCl and Fe(OH)3 on
ferrate solution stability. A signi®cant acceleration
effect was found for NaCl and Fe(OH)3 additions.
The increase in the second fast decomposition period
for solutions with a low ferrate starting concentration
was explained as being due to the accelerating effect of
the
Fe(OH)3, which
is a ferrate decomposition
product.
Wagner et al 3 studied the in¯uence of temperature,
ferrate and hydroxide solution concentration and also
day and arti®cial light intensity. The most important
factor was found to be temperature. The decrease in
ferrate solution stability with increasing initial ferrate
concentration
was
also
con®rmed.
Controversial
results were found for the hydroxide concentration
dependency, which showed the lowest ferrate stability
in 3M hydroxide. No in¯uence of light was found.
A systematic study of the problem was carried out by
JezÏowska-Trzebiatowska
and
WronÏska
and
co-
workers4±7 who postulated4 that ferrate decomposition
was governed by ®rst order kinetics with respect to
ferrate (eqn (1). After precipitation of some Fe(OH)3
from solution, they observed that the reaction order
was halved.

duced the idea of a homogeneous ferrate decomposi-

Ernst et al 5 found, in contrast, that the ferrate de-
composition rate increased after the initial Fe(OH)3,
precipitation, and explained this behaviour as being
caused by the catalytic in¯uence of Fe(OH)3, as
already suggested by Schreyer and Ockerman.2 Ernst
et al 5
also found, that the ferrate decomposition
kinetics could be slowed by the addition of PO3ÿ
4
ions.
It was also postulated that the Fe3 ion formed by
ferrate decomposition was incorporated into phos-
phoric complexes, thus preventing the formation of
the Fe(OH)3 solid phase.
According to TousÏek8 the stability of ferrate solution
initially increased with hydroxide concentration. Be-
yond a concentration of 20wt%, the decomposition
rate again increased. TousÏek explained this behaviour
as being due to the different properties of the Fe(OH)3
precipitate formed in hydroxide solutions of different
concentrations. At a concentration of 20wt% the
Fe(OH)3 precipitated on the vessel walls and did not
increase the liquid±solid interface area to catalyse
decomposition, thus leading to highest stability at this
concentration. According to TousÏek it is possible to
describe ferrate decomposition by an overall reaction
given by eqn (8).

8
The ferrate(III) formed reacts subsequently with water
according to eqn (9).
FeOÿ
2  2H2O ! Fe(OH)3  OHÿ
9
An alternative explanation was offered by Ettel and
VeprÏek-SÏisÏka9 who studied the in¯uence of Ni2 and
Co2 ions on ferrate stability. Concentrations of these
ions as low as 10ÿ5M resulted in a dramatic increase in
ferrate decomposition rate. It follows that even trace
impurities of Ni2 and/or Co2 ions in the hydroxide
solution may considerably modify the results obtained.
Ettel and VeprÏek-SÏisÏka9 also modi®ed the homo-
geneous ferrate decomposition mechanism proposed
by JezÏovska-Trzebiatowska and WronÏska.4 According
to these authors ferrate initially undergoes a one
electron reduction producing unstable ferrate(V),
which is further reduced in two subsequent steps.

More recently Denvir and Pletcher10 have suggested
that ferrate decomposition follows ®rst order kinetics
and have postulated an autocatalytic in¯uence of
Fe(OH)3 on the decomposition rate.
It was shown clearly in previous work that ferrate
decomposition is apparently in¯uenced by the con-
centration of iron existing in oxidation states lower
than Fe6 in the solution, especially if the solubility is
exceeded. It was shown11±13 that, for electrochemical
production of ferrate via anodic oxidation of iron, the
content of iron in lower oxidation stages in the anolyte
solution
differs
considerably
for
different
anode
material compositions. Highest ferrate current yield,
as well as the concentration of iron in lower oxidation
stages, was obtained using a white cast iron anode:
white cast iron has the highest iron carbide content.12
This was explained in terms of preferential iron
carbide dissolution during anodic polarisation accord-
ing to eqn (12).
Fe3C  18OHÿ ! 3FeOÿ
2  CO2ÿ
3
 9H2O  13eÿ
12
This continuous process is responsible for the local
disruption of the protective passive layer and the
formation of a thick, porous oxide layer enhancing
ferrate formation.14 The fate of the other minor
constituent elements of white cast iron anodes (Mn,
Ni) during ferrate formation is not known so far.
It is not yet known if the nature of the anode
material in¯uences the subsequent stability of the
ferrate solution produced. The aim of the present
study is to clarify this point.
2
EXPERIMENTAL
A 14M NaOH (Lachema, Praha, Czech Republic)
solution was made up from solid anhydrous NaOH
containing the following impurities (wt%): Cl 0.008,
Ag 0.002, Fe 0.002, Al 0.002, PO3ÿ
4
0.005, and Ca2
0.001.
A batch electrolyser described elsewhere11 was used
to prepare the sample solutions. Pure iron and white
cast iron anode materials were used. These two
materials
differ
signi®cantly in the iron carbide
content, their compositions being: `pure' 99.95wt%
Fe, containing less than 0.005wt%C, 0.0048wt% Ni
and 0.0003wt% Mn; `white cast iron' 3.16wt%C,
0.44wt% Mn and 0.036wt% Ni. The temperature
range 20±50°C and the anodic current density range
4.4±42.8mAcmÿ2 were studied.
After 180min of electrolysis the electrolyte was
drained and the solution was divided equally into
25cm3 vessels. Prior to each experiment all vessels
were carefully cleaned using a mixture of potassium
dichromate with concentrated sulfuric acid, rinsed
thoroughly in distilled water and dried. These vessels
were kept at the temperature of the preparative
electrolysis. At regular time intervals samples were
taken from the 25cm3 vessels for ferrate analysis using

After 180min of electrolysis the electrolyte was
drained and the solution was divided equally into
25cm3 vessels. Prior to each experiment all vessels
were carefully cleaned using a mixture of potassium
dichromate with concentrated sulfuric acid, rinsed
thoroughly in distilled water and dried. These vessels
were kept at the temperature of the preparative
electrolysis. At regular time intervals samples were
taken from the 25cm3 vessels for ferrate analysis using
J Chem Technol Biotechnol 74:1188±1194 (1999)
Stability of electrochemically-prepared ferrate(VI) solutions
the chromite method.15 The total iron concentration
was also estimated using the Reinhard±Zimmermann
To facilitate comparison of results obtained under
different conditions the actual value of the relative
decomposition rate given by eqn (13) was plotted
against time after the end of electrolysis. Although
experiments were performed over a wide current
density range, for economy and clarity of presentation,
only results for 12.5mAcmÿ2 (white cast iron) and
16.2mAcmÿ2 (pure iron) representing the middle of
the studied range, are shown in graphical form in Figs
Figure 1(A) shows that, at 20°C, an apparent
decrease in decomposition rate takes place within
approximately the ®rst 5000s of the end of electrolysis.
For the sample prepared using pure iron the decom-
position rate continues to decrease slightly. The
samples prepared using white cast iron show an
increase in decomposition rate following a minimum.
The decomposition rate for 30°C is shown in Fig
2(A). For white cast iron a decrease in the decom-
position rate occurs leading to a minimum after
approximately 5000±9000s, following which an in-
crease again occurs. With increasing current density
the period after which the minimum was reached
decreased. The sample solutions prepared using the
pure iron anode show a slow decrease in decomposi-
tion rate for all studied current densities.
Compared with 20 and 30°C pronounced differ-
ences are apparent at 40°C, as shown in Fig 3(A). As
well as an increase in the decomposition rate the shape
of the time dependence curve changes. A decrease in
decomposition rate following the end of the electro-
lysis occurs, followed by an increase for samples
prepared using both materials. The time of the
minimum for white cast iron decreased to approxi-
mately 4000±8000s. A maximum was followed by a
further decrease. For the sample prepared using pure
iron the minimum decomposition rate occurred at
2000±4000s from the end of electrolysis.
The decomposition rate for the highest temperature
of 50°C is shown in Fig 4(A). At this temperature the
time intervals after which the minimum and maximum
were reached again decreased. The decomposition rate
of the sample prepared using pure iron shows an
increase with time and after approximately 2000s it
exceeds the decomposition rate observed for samples
prepared using white cast iron. In contrast to the lower

represent the concentrations calculated using the ﬁrst order kinetic

concentration on storage time from the end of electrolysis, solution – 14M
NaOH, temperature – 30°C, samples produced using: * – white cast iron

the concentrations calculated using the ﬁrst order kinetic equation.
J Chem Technol Biotechnol 74:1188±1194 (1999)
K Bouzek, MJ Schmidt, AA Wragg
temperatures using pure iron the decomposition rate
increases at the beginning of the experiment. This is
due to the low iron carbide content in the anode
material and the consequently low concentrations of
Fe in lower oxidation states. This results in a relatively
slow increase in decomposition rate with temperature
within the ®rst 500s after the end of electrolysis.
Precipitation of the initial amounts of Fe(OH)3 takes
place at the beginning of storage at 50°C, resulting in
decomposition acceleration and a maximum occur-
Even these variations in ferrate decomposition rate
can be satisfactorily described by ®rst order kinetics.
This is demonstrated by Figs 1(B), 2(B), 3(B) and
4(B) showing the dependence of the ferrate concen-
tration on time from the end of electrolysis, together
with the line corresponding to the concentration
values calculated using ®rst order kinetics.
As already mentioned, the decomposition rate is
in¯uenced by Fe(OH)3 precipitation.2,5,8 Thus the
time-dependence of the total iron concentration was
followed parallel to that of the ferrate concentration to
provide information about Fe(OH)3 precipitation on
the walls of the storage vessel.
Figures 5±8 show the time dependencies of the total
iron concentration together with the concentration of
iron in oxidation states lower than Fe6 in solutions of
samples corresponding to Figs 1±4. Comparing Figs
1±4 and Figs 5±8 it is clear that there is a direct
connection between the increase in decomposition
rate and the time dependence of the concentrations of
total iron and iron in oxidation states lower than Fe6.
The results agree with the previous postulation
concerning the in¯uence of Fe(OH)3 on ferrate
decomposition rate.2,5,8,10 The strong decrease in
ferrate decomposition rate is followed by an increase
and a local maximum, see Figs 1(A), 2(A), 3(A) and
4(A). This maximum in decomposition rate corre-
sponds to a decrease in the total iron concentration in
the sample solution and/or to an increase in the
concentration of iron in oxidation states lower than
Fe6 (Figs 5±8). This is particularly apparent at
elevated temperature and for samples prepared using
white cast iron. This con®rms the conclusions of
TousÏek.8 The monotonic decrease in ferrate decom-
position rate following the local maximum corre-
sponds to decreasing ferrate concentration.
Even the ®rst order kinetics equation describes the
ferrate concentration change with storage time ade-
quately (Figs 1(B), 2(B), 3(B) and 4(B)). It follows
from Figs 1(A), 2(A), 3(A) and 4(A) that the mech-
anism of ferrate decomposition does not essentially

represent the concentrations calculated using the ﬁrst order kinetic

concentration on storage time from the end of electrolysis, solution – 14M
NaOH, temperature – 50°C, samples produced using: * – white cast iron
at 12.5mAcmÿ2 and * – pure iron at 16.2mAcmÿ2; the lines in (B)
represent the concentrations calculated using the ﬁrst order kinetic
J Chem Technol Biotechnol 74:1188±1194 (1999)
Stability of electrochemically-prepared ferrate(VI) solutions
follow this mechanism. Therefore, in using this
equation it is necessary to take into account the fact
that the kinetic constant is dependent not only on the
sample temperature, but also on the starting concen-
tration of ferrate and iron in lower oxidation states.
The kinetic constants for the individual cases were
calculated using the last squares method. For the
reasons stated above the kinetic constants summarised
in Table 1 are valid only for the conditions under
which they were obtained. Signi®cant deviation from
the electrolysis time, anode material composition,
current density of NaOH concentration, which would
in¯uence the ferrate concentration achieved, as well as
the concentration of iron in oxidation states lower than
Fe6, will result in changes in the kinetic constants.
Table 1 shows that the decomposition rate is faster
for solutions prepared using white cast iron than for
those prepared with pure iron, even though the initial
ferrate concentration is higher at the elevated tem-
perature in the samples prepared using pure iron. This
phenomenon is caused by the higher concentration of
iron in oxidation states lower than Fe6 in solutions
prepared by electrolysis using white cast iron.11±13
This behaviour highlights the difference between
solutions prepared using these two materials.
Even though the decomposition rate of ferrate
prepared using white cast iron is higher, this material
provides economically better results for the following
reasons. White cast iron provides the highest ferrate
yield at 20°C. This is a conveniently low temperature
compared with the 50°C necessary to reach compar-
able results using a pure iron anode. Moreover, at
50°C the decomposition rate of ferrate solutions
prepared using pure iron is approximately 10 times
higher than that for solutions prepared at 20°C using
The in¯uence of the composition of the anode material
used in electrochemical ferrate preparation on the
subsequent solution stability was studied. It was found
that increasing iron carbide content of the anode
material enhances ferrate decomposition kinetics. This
is explained by the higher concentration of iron in
oxidation states lower than Fe6 in solutions prepared
using material with higher iron carbide content (white
cast iron). Nevertheless, the use of anode materials
with high iron carbide content is economically favour-
able. This is due to the increase in the electrolyte
temperature necessary to reach a comparable ferrate
concentration in the anolyte at the same current
density with an anode of low iron carbide content.
Increase in temperature has a greater accelerating
effect on decomposition rate than increase in iron
